candidates.name: Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-074MR Court finds Sunshine Loans charged customers prohibited fees
SunshineLoans Pty Ltd v Australian Securities and Investments Commission [2026] HCA 8
25-046MR Full Federal Court finds in favour of ASIC in two appeals concerning Sunshine Loans
26-052MR High Court finds in favour of ASIC on an appeal by Sunshine Loans

candidates.public_interest_reason: Federal Court found Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd contravened the National Credit Code and National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 by charging a prohibited $35 'amendment fee' on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts between 1 July 2016 and 2 November 2020, in proceedings brought by ASIC seeking $10.5 million in penalties. The company's application to have the trial judge recuse himself from the penalty hearing (on apprehended-bias grounds) was dismissed by the High Court of Australia on 18 March 2026 ([2026] HCA 8). Following the trial judge's recusal from the penalty phase (on apprehended-bias grounds relating to his credibility findings against Sunshine Loans and its witnesses), ASIC successfully appealed that recusal decision to the Full Federal Court (March 2025) and then, after Sunshine Loans' further appeal, to the High Court, which found unanimously (7-0) in ASIC's favour on 18 March 2026. The penalty hearing has now been remitted to the original trial judge, Justice Roger Derrington, for determination.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have contravened the National Credit Code/NCCP Act by charging a prohibited fee on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts
Subject of ASIC penalty proceedings seeking $10.5 million

candidates.name: United Super Pty Ltd (trustee for Cbus)

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Cbus and launches investigation
25-286MR Cbus ordered to pay $23.5 million penalty for serious failures in processing members death benefits and insurance claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from United Super Pty Ltd, trustee for the Construction and Building Unions Superannuation Fund (Cbus, over 920,000 member accounts and around $100 billion in funds under management), after a prudential review found significant and persistent weaknesses in its operational risk management framework across four areas: operational risk management, information/IT resilience, insurance claims processing and administration, and expenditure management. Undertaking spans four areas: operational risk management, IT resilience, insurance claims administration, and expenditure management
Separately under APRA investigation for possible breaches of the SIS Act relating to expenditure management practices

candidates.name: Reebelo Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Reebelo Australia pays penalties for alleged false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights

candidates.public_interest_reason: Reebelo Australia, an online marketplace for new and refurbished electronics, paid $59,400 in penalties after the ACCC issued three infringement notices alleging false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights. The statements purported to impose a 14-day time limit on consumers' ability to obtain a remedy for faulty, damaged, misdescribed or wrong-model goods, when the Australian Consumer Law entitles consumers to a remedy regardless of elapsed time. The ACCC noted particular concern given the products involved are often refurbished high-end electronics such as laptops and mobile phones.

candidates.name: EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: AER Infringement Notices - EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd - INF-20260001 and INF-20260002
EnergyAustralia to pay $14m for making misleading statements and breaching the Electricity Retail Code

candidates.public_interest_reason: EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd paid two AER infringement notices totalling $142,800 for alleged breaches of the National Energy Retail Law and National Energy Retail Rules. The AER alleged EnergyAustralia transferred an elderly customer's electricity account to itself without obtaining consent (after another person had mistakenly set up an account at that address), then sent bills and disconnection warnings, and disconnected the customer without following the required notice procedures under the Retail Rules. Separately, the Federal Court ordered EnergyAustralia to pay $14 million in penalties (the ACCC's first proceeding under the Electricity Retail Code since its 2019 introduction) after EnergyAustralia admitted sending misleading price-change communications to around 566,000 consumers between June and September 2022, including misrepresenting the estimated annual price of its electricity offer for an average customer and failing to state the lowest possible price, plus 27 non-compliant online electricity offers over the same period.
risk_indicators: Paid $142,800 across two AER infringement notices for alleged Retail Law and Retail Rules breaches

candidates.name: Origin Energy Electricity Ltd

source_evidence.title: AER v Origin Energy Electricity Ltd & Ors
Origin penalised $17 million for customer hardship breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: The AER instituted Federal Court proceedings on 19 December 2025 against Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and three other Origin subsidiaries, alleging the group continued to receive Centrepay payments from customers who had closed their accounts and fully paid what they owed, without informing or refunding them within the required timeframe — around 77,000 alleged breaches affecting over 3,400 customers between December 2019 and March 2025, totalling more than $2.5 million in retained overcharges. The AER also alleges Origin knew of the processing issue as early as 2017 and paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging, and separately failed to have adequate compliance-monitoring policies and systems in place. The AER is seeking penalties, declarations, customer remediation and a compliance program. Separately, the Federal Court found Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and other Origin entities breached hardship-customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Law/Rules on more than 100,000 occasions between January 2018 and October 2021, affecting more than 90,000 customers across NSW, the ACT, Queensland and South Australia, resulting in a $17 million penalty — the largest total penalty ever imposed for breaches of that law at the time — plus $200,000 in costs and a compliance program.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings instituted by the AER on 19 December 2025, alleging around 77,000 breaches of the National Energy Retail Rules/Law
Alleged to have retained more than $2.5 million in Centrepay overcharges from over 3,400 affected customers
Alleged to have known of the systems issue as early as 2017 and to have paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging
AER separately alleges a failure to have adequate policies, systems and procedures to monitor Retail Rules compliance

The penalty comprised $4.2 million for failing to take reasonable steps to protect personal information (APP 11.1), $800,000 for failing to carry out a reasonable and expeditious assessment of whether an eligible data breach had occurred, and $800,000 for failing to properly notify the Australian Information Commissioner of the breach.

candidates.name: Risham Nominees Pty Ltd (trading as Centenary Bakehouse)

source_evidence.title: Bakery fined record $3.4 million for workplace manslaughter

candidates.public_interest_reason: Risham Nominees Pty Ltd, trading as Centenary Bakehouse, was convicted of workplace manslaughter - only the second such conviction under Victoria's workplace manslaughter provisions since their 2020 introduction - and fined $3.4 million, a record penalty, over the death of worker Jassim Al Shadori, who fell approximately 4.5 metres through a roof space while removing insulation panels at the company's Reservoir bakery on 1 August 2021. WorkSafe Victoria found no fall-prevention controls had been applied before the roof works began, despite harnesses being available but not required to be used.
risk_indicators: Convicted of workplace manslaughter, only the second such conviction in Victoria since the offence was introduced in 2020
Fined $3.4 million, a record penalty for the offence
WorkSafe found no fall-prevention controls were required or applied despite equipment being available

candidates.name: Pearl Construction Group Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Builder fined over worker's fatal fall

candidates.public_interest_reason: Pearl Construction Group Pty Ltd pleaded guilty to failing to ensure a workplace under its management and control was safe and without risks to health, and was fined $150,000 in the Melbourne County Court, following the death of a 23-year-old worker who fell approximately three metres through an unprotected stair void while carrying steel at a multi-storey townhouse site in Glen Waverley in September 2022. A subcontractor sole trader involved in the same incident was separately sentenced to a community corrections order plus a $10,000 fine.
risk_indicators: Pleaded guilty to failing to ensure a safe workplace following a worker's fatal fall
Fined $150,000 by the Melbourne County Court
Incident involved an unprotected stair void with no fall-prevention measures in place

candidates.name: Amazon Commercial Services Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Amazon in court for introducing ads to Prime Video using allegedly unfair contract terms

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings on 29 June 2026 alleging Amazon Commercial Services Pty Ltd included five unfair terms in its standard-form Prime subscription contracts, held by more than one million annual subscribers between November 2023 and August 2025, letting it unilaterally degrade services or alter conditions without pro-rata refunds. The ACCC further alleges Amazon relied on these terms in July 2024 to introduce advertising into Prime Video without compensating subscribers, who then had to pay an extra $2.99 per month to remain ad-free. This is among the first contested cases under Australia's unfair contract terms civil penalty regime.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the ACCC alleging unfair contract terms, ongoing
Alleged to have relied on contested contract terms to introduce advertising without compensating over one million subscribers
Among the first contested cases under Australia's unfair contract terms civil penalty regime

candidates.name: Mastermyne Crinum Operations Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: $7M penalty in first industrial manslaughter conviction

candidates.public_interest_reason: Mastermyne Crinum Operations Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of the Metarock Group, was found guilty by jury of industrial manslaughter under Queensland's Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999 - the first such conviction since the offence took effect in 2020 - following the death of underground miner Graham Dawson in a roof collapse at the Crinum coal mine on 14 September 2021. Resources Safety and Health Queensland found the company's strata control systems were inadequate. The District Court of Queensland imposed a $7 million fine plus costs, Australia's largest-ever work health and safety penalty; the company has lodged a notice of appeal.
risk_indicators: First-ever conviction for industrial manslaughter under Queensland's Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999
Fined $7 million plus costs, described as Australia's largest-ever work health and safety penalty
Regulator found the company's strata control systems were inadequate
Company has lodged a notice of appeal against the conviction

candidates.name: Bureau Veritas Minerals Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Crusher incident leads to $210,000 fine

candidates.public_interest_reason: Bureau Veritas Minerals Pty Ltd pleaded guilty to a charge under South Australia's Work Health and Safety Act 2012 and was fined $210,000 (reduced 40% from $350,000 for an early guilty plea) plus costs and a Victims of Crime Levy, after a 20-year-old worker employed only a few weeks had a gloved hand pulled into a rock crusher's rollers at a Wingfield workshop in June 2023, amputating part of a finger. SafeWork SA found the company failed to provide an adequate interlock device, failed to assess energisation risk during clearing tasks, and gave inadequate training and supervision.
risk_indicators: Pleaded guilty to a work health and safety offence following a worker's serious crush injury
Regulator found the company failed to provide an adequate machine interlock device and gave inadequate training and supervision
Fined $210,000 plus costs by the SA Employment Court

candidates.name: Oztures Trading Pty Ltd (trading as Binance Australia Derivatives)

source_evidence.title: 26-055MR Binance Australia Derivatives ordered to pay $10 million penalty for onboarding failures causing millions in client trading losses

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Oztures Trading Pty Ltd, trading as Binance Australia Derivatives, to pay a $10 million penalty (orders handed down 27 March 2026) after it admitted misclassifying more than 85% of its Australian client base - 524 retail investors - as wholesale clients between July 2022 and April 2023, exposing them to high-risk crypto derivatives products without the consumer protections required for retail clients. The misclassified clients suffered approximately $8.66 million in trading losses and paid approximately $3.89 million in fees; ASIC separately oversaw approximately $13.1 million in compensation to affected clients in 2023.
risk_indicators: Admitted misclassifying over 85% of its Australian client base as wholesale rather than retail clients
Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $10 million penalty
Misclassified clients suffered a combined $8.66 million in trading losses and paid $3.89 million in fees

candidates.name: Qantas Airways Limited

source_evidence.title: Federal Court orders Qantas to pay $100m in penalties for misleading consumers

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Qantas Airways Limited to pay $100 million in penalties (8 October 2024) for misleading 86,597 members of the public by selling tickets on flights it had already decided to cancel, and misleading 883,977 people by not promptly notifying them of flight cancellations. Qantas separately agreed to pay approximately $20 million in compensation to affected passengers ($225 for domestic/trans-Tasman flights, $450 for international flights, per eligible passenger).
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $100 million penalty for misleading consumers
Found to have sold tickets on flights already decided to be cancelled, affecting 86,597 members of the public
Found to have delayed cancellation notifications to 883,977 people
Separately committed approximately $20 million to a customer compensation program

candidates.name: NDN Care Services Pty Ltd (trading as Della Dale Aged Care)

source_evidence.title: Statement from Liz Hefren-Webb, Aged Care Quality and Safety Commissioner - Revocation of Approved Provider Status of NDN Care Services Pty Ltd (Della Dale Aged Care), Ringwood (VIC)

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Aged Care Quality and Safety Commission revoked the approved provider status of NDN Care Services Pty Ltd, trading as Della Dale Aged Care in Ringwood, Victoria - its most severe regulatory sanction - after finding all 19 residents were prescribed psychotropic medication without proper management, planning or monitoring as required by the Aged Care Quality Standards, and that an 'extreme form of physical restraint' had been used on a resident without consideration of their safety or wellbeing. The notice was issued in May 2025, with the revocation taking effect 26 September 2025.
risk_indicators: Approved provider status revoked by the Aged Care Quality and Safety Commission, its most severe sanction
Found to have prescribed psychotropic medication to all residents without proper management or monitoring
Found to have used an extreme form of physical restraint on a resident without regard to their safety or wellbeing
